title: Mobile Claude and voice
subtitle: Apps for iOS and Android, voice mode and camera: Claude always within reach

sources.title: Claude on Mobile — Claude Help Center

theory.md:
Everything you've learned in this world works not just at your computer. Claude has **official mobile apps for iOS and Android** — search for "Claude" in the App Store or Google Play (the publisher is Anthropic; be careful — popular apps sometimes have fake clones).

Sign in to the same account and you get a **shared chat history**: a conversation you started on your computer, you can continue on your phone on the subway, and vice versa. Started sketching out a presentation plan over breakfast in the morning — you pick it up at your desk right where you left off.

The app's interface mirrors the web version: an input field, chat history, search, model selection. But your phone has two superpowers that a computer doesn't: **a microphone right at hand** and **a camera**.

theory.text: Voice mode: talk instead of typing
Camera: show Claude what you see

theory.md:
Mobile Claude is valuable not because it's "the same thing, but on a phone," but because it's right there in the moments when there's no computer but there is a question. A few examples of how this fits into your day.

**Morning:** on your way to work, talk through your day's plan by voice — "I have three meetings and a report, help me set priorities." **Daytime:** in a store, photograph two products and compare ingredients. **Evening:** while cooking dinner from a recipe, ask by voice "what can I substitute for cream?" — without touching the screen with messy hands. **On a trip:** a photo of a schedule, a sign, or a ticket — "what time is my train and which platform?"

The general principle: previously such micro-tasks were either postponed "until you're at a computer" or solved with half an hour of googling. Now, between the question and the answer, there's one photo or one phrase.

rows: Cooking dinner, hands covered in flour
"What can I substitute for cream in this recipe?"
Photo of labels: "Compare the ingredients, which is healthier?"
"Explain topic X, I have 20 minutes" — listen and ask follow-ups
Photo of the menu: "Translate it, what here has no meat?"
An idea struck on a walk
Dictate the thought, ask it to be shaped into a note
A child asks "why is the sky blue"
Figure it out together with Claude out loud

theory.bad: Standing in the store, trying to manually retype the tiny ingredient list from the label into the chat: "watersflourssaltemulsifier E471…" — with typos, five minutes, and you couldn't make out half of it.
theory.good: Photograph the label into the chat and ask: "I'm trying to avoid sugar in hidden forms. Is there any here, and under what names?"
theory.explanation: The camera removes an entire layer of manual work: no need to retype — Claude reads the text from the photo itself, even the fine print. And a specific question (not "what is this?" but "find the hidden sugar") gives you an actionable answer right there at the store shelf.

theory.md: The Claude Help Center (support.claude.com, the Claude on Mobile section) confirms: the official apps are available for iOS and Android, support voice conversations with Claude, uploading photos and files, and chat history syncs with the web version — you can continue conversations from any device.

Try the "one-week rule": every time a question comes up on the go that you'd normally google or postpone — ask Claude by voice or with a photo. Over a week you'll discover your own personal scenarios that no lesson describes.

Voice and camera are still sending data into the chat. Don't photograph other people's documents or dictate sensitive information (passwords, full card details) — same as in the web version. And don't use your phone while driving: voice mode only via hands-free and by the rules.

tasks.question: What happens to a chat you started on your computer if you open the Claude mobile app with the same account?
options: It's in the history — you can continue the conversation from your phone right where you left off
The history is empty on the phone: computer chats don't get there
You'll have to email the chat to yourself
The mobile app doesn't show history at all
tasks.explanation: Chat history is shared across all devices on one account: started on the computer — continued on the phone on the subway, and vice versa. This is one of the main convenient little things about mobile Claude.
tasks.hint: The key phrase from the theory — "shared history" between devices.

tasks.question: In which situations is voice mode more convenient than typing? Select all that apply.
options: You want to practice conversational foreign language
You need to send Claude a photo of a document
You want to listen to an explanation of a topic on the way, asking follow-ups as you go
tasks.explanation: Voice helps when your hands are busy, for speech practice, and for a "podcast with feedback" on the way. But a photo is sent by the camera, not by voice — that's the phone's other superpower.
tasks.hint: One of the options is about an image, not about speech.

tasks.instruction: A mobile scenario simulation. You're in a supermarket choosing breakfast cereal, and you've photographed the ingredient label (the photo is already "attached"). Your goal — less sugar. Ask Claude about these ingredients. Then clarify what to buy instead.

steps.failHint: Ask about what concerns you, for example: "How much sugar is here? I'm trying to avoid it."

steps.claudeReply:
Here's what to look for on the shelf — right now, while you're in the store:

1. **Old-fashioned rolled oats** — a one-word ingredient list: "rolled oats." Ideal.
2. **Muesli with no added sugar** — look for "no sugar" on the package, but still check the ingredients: there should be no syrups.
3. Add the sweetness yourself: a banana or berries — that's a completely different story than syrup in the ingredients.

A rule for the future: if sugar (or syrup/honey/molasses) is in the first three ingredients — put it back on the shelf. Happy shopping! 🛒
steps.failHint: Ask for an alternative, for example: "So what should I get instead of these?"

tasks.successMessage: Great! Photo + a specific question + a follow-up — and the decision is made right at the shelf in a minute. That's mobile Claude in action.

tasks.instruction: A beginner's notes about mobile Claude. Find the incorrect statement.
lines: 1. Claude apps exist for both iOS and Android.
2. In the app you can talk with Claude by voice.
3. You can photograph a document or label straight into the chat.
4. Voice mode is only in the web version on a computer; the app doesn't have it.
5. Chat history syncs between phone and computer.
tasks.explanation: The mistake is on line 4 — it's exactly the opposite: voice mode is precisely a feature of the mobile apps. The rest is correct: apps on both platforms, the camera for photos into the chat, and shared history across devices.

tasks.instruction:
Mission: put Claude in your pocket.

1. Install the official **Claude** app from the App Store or Google Play (the publisher is Anthropic) and sign in to your account.
2. Make sure you can see your chats from the computer — open one of them.
3. Try **voice mode**: find the voice conversation button in the app and ask something out loud — for example, ask for a dinner idea. Ask a follow-up question by voice.
4. Try the **camera**: photograph any label, book cover, or (non-confidential) document into the chat and ask a question about the photo.
5. Bonus: start any chat on your phone and continue it on your computer.
checklist: I installed the official app and signed in
I can see my computer chats in the app
I talked with Claude by voice and asked a follow-up
I sent a photo into the chat and got an answer about it
(Bonus) I continued a mobile chat on the computer
